posts.title: SO(3) and SE(3): the groups underneath every rotation
posts.summary: What makes SO(3) a group, the tangent space and exponential map, exactly why Euler angles hit gimbal lock at pitch = 90 degrees, the SU(2) double cover, and how SE(3) extends all of it to rigid motion.
posts.body:
Every time a robot arm plans a motion, a camera pose gets estimated, or a molecule gets rotated into a canonical frame, something underneath is quietly relying on the fact that rotations and rigid motions form a group. The group structure isn't decoration, it's what makes composition, interpolation, and optimization over rotations well-defined instead of ad hoc.

## SO(3): the rotation group

$SO(3)$ is the set of $3 \times 3$ real matrices that preserve lengths and orientation:

$$
SO(3) = \left\{\, R \in \mathbb{R}^{3\times3} \;\middle|\; R^{\top}R = I,\ \det R = 1 \,\right\}.
$$

$R^\top R = I$ makes $R$ orthogonal, it preserves dot products, so lengths and angles between vectors don't change under $R$. $\det R = 1$ rather than $-1$ rules out reflections, an orthogonal matrix with determinant $-1$ flips handedness, which is a valid isometry but not a rotation you could reach by continuously turning something.

Checking this is a group is short and worth doing once: the identity matrix is in the set, the product of two such matrices is again orthogonal with determinant $1$ (closure), matrix multiplication is associative for free, and $R^{-1} = R^\top$, which is itself in $SO(3)$, gives inverses. What makes $SO(3)$ more than an abstract group is that it's also a smooth manifold, a *Lie group*, curved in a specific way and three-dimensional (three numbers, an axis and an angle, specify any rotation).

## The tangent space and the exponential map

Because $SO(3)$ is a smooth manifold, it has a tangent space at every point, and the tangent space at the identity, written $\mathfrak{so}(3)$, is the set of $3\times3$ skew-symmetric matrices. Every skew-symmetric matrix corresponds to a 3-vector via the hat map:

$$
\omega = (\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3) \quad \longmapsto \quad
[\omega]_\times =
\begin{pmatrix}
0 & -\omega_3 & \omega_2 \\
\omega_3 & 0 & -\omega_1 \\
-\omega_2 & \omega_1 & 0
\end{pmatrix},
$$

chosen so that $[\omega]_\times v = \omega \times v$ for any vector $v$, matrix multiplication by $[\omega]_\times$ acts exactly like the cross product with $\omega$. The matrix exponential maps this tangent space back onto the group, and for $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ it has a closed form, Rodrigues' rotation formula: for a unit axis $k$ and angle $\theta$,

$$
R = \exp\big(\theta [k]_\times\big) = I + \sin\theta\, [k]_\times + (1-\cos\theta)\, [k]_\times^2.
$$

This is the bridge between "a rotation" (an element of $SO(3)$) and "an angular velocity applied for one unit of time" (an element of the tangent space), and it's the reason axis-angle representations, exponential coordinates, and Lie-algebra-based optimization all show up constantly in robotics and vision code: optimizing directly on the manifold means taking small steps in $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ and exponentiating back, rather than parametrizing rotations with something that has spurious degrees of freedom or singularities.

```python
import numpy as np

def rodrigues(axis, theta):
    k = axis / np.linalg.norm(axis)
    K = np.array([[0, -k[2], k[1]],
                  [k[2], 0, -k[0]],
                  [-k[1], k[0], 0]])
    I = np.eye(3)
    return I + np.sin(theta) * K + (1 - np.cos(theta)) * (K @ K)
```

## Where Euler angles go wrong

A common alternative to axis-angle is three Euler angles (roll, pitch, yaw), which is intuitive but not a chart that covers $SO(3)$ without a singularity. At pitch $= \pm 90°$, the roll and yaw axes become parallel and one degree of freedom is lost, this is gimbal lock. It shows up as the determinant of the Jacobian mapping Euler angle rates to angular velocity going to zero, and for the standard ZYX convention that determinant is exactly $\cos(\text{pitch})$:

```plot
{
  "data": [
    {"x": [-90, -75, -60, -45, -30, -15, 0, 15, 30, 45, 60, 75, 90],
     "y": [0.0, 0.2588, 0.5, 0.7071, 0.866, 0.9659, 1.0, 0.9659, 0.866, 0.7071, 0.5, 0.2588, 0.0],
     "type": "scatter", "mode": "lines+markers", "name": "cos(pitch), Euler-rate Jacobian determinant"}
  ],
  "layout": {
    "title": "Gimbal lock: exact Jacobian determinant vs pitch angle",
    "xaxis": {"title": "pitch (degrees)"},
    "yaxis": {"title": "determinant"}
  }
}
```

At $\pm 90°$ the determinant hits zero exactly, the parametrization becomes degenerate there regardless of how the angles are numerically represented. Quaternions and axis-angle representations don't have a coordinate singularity like this, which is the real, mechanical reason they're preferred over Euler angles in anything that has to integrate or optimize through a wide range of orientations.

## The double cover: SU(2) and quaternions

Unit quaternions, $SU(2)$, map onto $SO(3)$ two-to-one: a quaternion $q$ and its negation $-q$ represent the same rotation. This isn't a nuisance to work around so much as the reason quaternions avoid the coordinate singularities that plague three-parameter representations, $SU(2)$ is simply connected (any loop can be contracted to a point) while $SO(3)$ is not, and a rotation by $360°$ traces a loop in $SO(3)$ that is *not* contractible, you have to go around twice, back to the same quaternion, to get back to a contractible loop. This is the same topological fact behind the Dirac belt trick and why a spin-$\tfrac12$ particle needs a $720°$ rotation to return to its original state.

```mermaid
graph TD
SU2["SU(2), unit quaternions"] -->|"$$2:1$$"| SO3["SO(3), rotations"]
SO3 --> SE3["SE(3), rotation + translation"]
R3["$$\mathbb{R}^3$$, translations"] --> SE3
```

## SE(3): adding translation

$SE(3)$, the special Euclidean group, combines a rotation with a translation. It's not simply the product $SO(3) \times \mathbb{R}^3$, because rotating first and then translating is a different operation from translating first and then rotating, the group is a semidirect product $\mathbb{R}^3 \rtimes SO(3)$. In practice it's represented as a $4\times4$ homogeneous matrix,

$$
T =
\begin{pmatrix}
R & t \\
0 & 1
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad R \in SO(3),\ t \in \mathbb{R}^3,
$$

so that composing two rigid motions is a single matrix multiplication, $T_1 T_2$, and applying $T$ to a point is one matrix-vector product against the point in homogeneous coordinates. The tangent space $\mathfrak{se}(3)$ pairs an angular velocity with a linear velocity (a twist, in robotics terminology), and the same exponential map story as $SO(3)$ carries over: interpolate and optimize in the tangent space, exponentiate back onto the group when a valid rigid motion is actually needed. This is exactly the mechanism behind the [orientation fusion project](post.html?slug=geodesic-averaging-demo), averaging in $\mathfrak{so}(3)$ or $\mathfrak{se}(3)$ rather than averaging matrices or homogeneous coordinates directly is what keeps the result on the manifold instead of producing something that isn't a valid rotation or rigid motion at all.

posts.title: A short history of learning where things go: hand-crafted features to geometry-aware embeddings
posts.summary: From SIFT and bag-of-words, through word2vec and distributional semantics, to contrastive and masked self-supervision, to choosing embedding geometry (hyperbolic, equivariant) on purpose. Same question throughout: what should the representation space look like.
posts.body:
"Learning a good representation" has meant something different every decade, but there's a thread running through all of it: a representation is good when the geometry of the embedding space reflects some geometry that actually exists in the data, distance, similarity, hierarchy, symmetry, whatever the task needs preserved. The history of representation learning is largely a history of getting better at choosing what geometry to preserve, and how to preserve it without hand-designing it. ```mermaid graph LR A["hand-crafted features
SIFT, HOG, bag-of-words"] --> B["distributed vectors
word2vec, GloVe, ~2013-2014"] B --> C["self-supervised contrastive
CPC, SimCLR, MoCo, ~2018-2020"] C --> D["masked / generative pretext
BERT, MAE, ~2018-2021"] D --> E["geometry-aware embeddings
hyperbolic, equivariant, ongoing"] ``` ## Before learning: hand-designed geometry Early computer vision and NLP pipelines designed the geometry by hand. SIFT and HOG features were built so that Euclidean distance between descriptors meant something about visual similarity, engineered invariance to lighting, scale, or small shifts, baked directly into the feature by construction rather than learned. Bag-of-words text representations did something structurally similar: cosine similarity between sparse count vectors was meant to track topical similarity. These worked, within their limits, precisely because a human had already decided what "similar" should mean and encoded it directly. ## Distributed representations: let similarity emerge from prediction Word2vec's skip-gram model reframed the problem: instead of hand-defining similarity, train an embedding so that words appearing in similar contexts end up nearby, by making each word predict its neighbors. The distributional hypothesis, that words used in similar contexts tend to have similar meaning, is much older than word2vec, what changed was that a simple prediction objective, optimized at scale, produced embeddings where linear structure in the vector space tracked real semantic relationships (the classic king − man + woman ≈ queen observation) that nobody explicitly designed the geometry to support. The representation's geometry became a side effect of a much simpler objective: predict what's nearby. ## Contrastive and masked objectives: prediction without labels The next shift generalized "predict what's nearby" past language. Contrastive methods like CPC and SimCLR train an encoder so that two augmented views of the same input end up close in embedding space while views of different inputs end up far apart, no labels required, the supervision comes from knowing which pairs came from the same source. Masked modeling, BERT for text and later MAE for images, instead hides part of the input and trains the model to reconstruct or predict it, forcing the representation to capture enough structure to fill in what's missing. Both are self-supervised: the label comes from the data's own structure (an augmentation pairing, or a masking pattern) rather than from human annotation, which is most of why these methods scale, the "labels" are free and effectively unlimited. ## Geometry becomes an explicit design choice again The most recent shift treats the geometry of the embedding space itself as something to choose deliberately, rather than defaulting to flat Euclidean space and hoping the model figures out the right structure inside it. Hierarchical data, taxonomies, trees, is a clean example: the number of nodes within radius $r$ of the root of a tree grows exponentially with $r$ once branching factor exceeds one, and Euclidean space simply can't accommodate that, distances between siblings and distances between a node and a distant ancestor end up compressed against each other because Euclidean volume only grows polynomially. Hyperbolic space has the opposite property, area (and volume) grows exponentially with radius, which is a closer geometric match to how a tree actually grows: ```plot { "data": [ {"x": [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "y": [0.0, 3.14, 12.57, 28.27, 50.27, 78.54, 113.1], "type": "scatter", "mode": "lines+markers", "name": "Euclidean disk area, \u03c0r\u00b2"}, {"x": [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "y": [0.0, 3.41, 17.36, 56.97, 165.3, 459.99, 1261.13], "type": "scatter", "mode": "lines+markers", "name": "hyperbolic disk area, curvature -1"} ], "layout": { "title": "Area growth with radius: Euclidean (polynomial) vs hyperbolic (exponential)", "xaxis": {"title": "radius r"}, "yaxis": {"title": "enclosed area", "type": "log"} } } ``` Poincaré embeddings, mapping hierarchical data into hyperbolic space instead of Euclidean space, exploit exactly this, a tree with branching factor 10 needs exponentially more room than a Euclidean embedding can offer at any fixed dimension, and hyperbolic space has that room built in at the level of the metric itself, not as something the model has to learn to approximate. Equivariant representations, keeping rotation, permutation, or gauge structure explicit instead of flattening it into an ordinary vector, are the same move in a different direction: rather than asking a flat embedding space to somehow encode a symmetry it has no natural way to represent, the representation is built in a space (a group, a manifold) that already has the right structure. ## The pattern across all of it Every step in this history swaps out an assumption about where a representation's geometry should come from. Hand-crafted features assumed a human should define it. Distributed representations assumed simple prediction objectives at scale would produce it as a side effect. Contrastive and masked pretraining assumed the data's own structure could supply the supervision needed to learn it, without hand-labeling. The current move assumes something narrower and, in hindsight, more obvious: if you already know the right geometry, hierarchical, rotational, permutation-symmetric, build the representation in a space that has that geometry natively, instead of asking a flat vector space and enough data to discover it from scratch. That's the same principle behind the [receptive-field and equivariance discussion](post.html?slug=beyond-the-grid-geometric-deep-learning) and the [SO(3)/SE(3) note](post.html?slug=so3-se3-elegant-groups), representation learning and geometric deep learning aren't really separate subfields, they're the same question, asked from opposite ends: what should the space look like, and how do you get data into it without breaking that structure.

posts.title: Beyond the grid: a short tour of geometric deep learning
posts.summary: Convolutions assume a grid. Graphs, point clouds, and meshes don't have one. A walkthrough of equivariance, permutation-invariant message passing, receptive field growth on real vs small-world topology, and where rotation equivariance connects back to the orientation-fusion project.
posts.body:
Most of what deep learning is good at, convolutional nets on images, transformers on token sequences, quietly assumes the data sits on a grid or a sequence. Graphs, meshes, molecules, and point clouds don't come with that structure for free. Geometric deep learning is the attempt to define "convolution" precisely enough that it still means something once the grid is gone, and building blocks like the fusion layer in the [orientation project](research.html) turn out to be one instance of the same idea.

## Why the grid was ever special

A convolutional filter slides across an image because shifting the image and then filtering gives the same result as filtering and then shifting. If $T_v$ denotes translating by a vector $v$ and $f$ is a conv layer, that property is written

$$
f(T_v x) = T_v f(x) \quad \text{for all } v.
$$

This is called *equivariance*. Weight sharing across a convolution isn't a convenient trick, it's forced by this identity: the same filter has to work at every position because "position" is defined by the translation group acting on the image.

The general definition drops the specific group. For $G$ acting on an input space $X$ and an output space $Y$, $f: X \to Y$ is $G$-equivariant if

$$
f(g \cdot x) = g \cdot f(x) \quad \forall\, g \in G,\ x \in X,
$$

and *invariant* in the special case where $G$ acts trivially on $Y$, so $f(g \cdot x) = f(x)$ regardless of $g$. A classifier should usually be invariant to whatever symmetry its input has (a rotated cat is still a cat), while an intermediate feature extractor should usually be equivariant (rotate the input, the features rotate the same way, nothing is thrown away).

Different data types carry different natural groups:

| Domain | Symmetry group | What the group does |
|---|---|---|
| Images | translations, $\mathbb{Z}^2$ | shift pixels |
| Graphs | permutations, $S_n$ | relabel nodes |
| Point clouds, molecules | $SE(3)$ | rotate and translate in 3D |
| Meshes, manifolds | gauge transformations | change the local coordinate frame at each point |

This table is close to the taxonomy Bronstein, Bronstein, Cohen, and Veličković lay out in their geometric deep learning blueprint, that framing is theirs, not something original to this post, credit where it's due.

## Permutation equivariance, concretely

A graph neural network layer updates each node from its neighbors:

$$
h_v^{(k+1)} = \phi\!\left(h_v^{(k)},\ \bigoplus_{u \,\in\, \mathcal{N}(v)} \psi\!\left(h_v^{(k)}, h_u^{(k)}\right)\right)
$$

$\psi$ computes a message per neighbor, $\bigoplus$ aggregates them, $\phi$ combines the aggregate with the node's own state. The reason $\bigoplus$ has to be something like sum, mean, or max, and never anything that depends on the order the neighbors happen to be listed in, is permutation equivariance: relabel every node in the graph with some permutation $\pi$, and the layer's output at node $\pi(v)$ has to be exactly what the output at $v$ was before relabeling. Concatenating neighbor features in list order would break that immediately, since the "list order" of a node's neighbors isn't a real property of the graph, it's an artifact of how the adjacency list happens to be stored.

```mermaid
graph LR
U1["neighbor u1"] -->|"$$\psi(h_v,h_{u_1})$$"| AGG(("$$\bigoplus$$"))
U2["neighbor u2"] -->|"$$\psi(h_v,h_{u_2})$$"| AGG
U3["neighbor u3"] -->|"$$\psi(h_v,h_{u_3})$$"| AGG
AGG --> PHI["$$\phi(h_v, \cdot)$$"]
V["node v, current state"] --> PHI
PHI --> OUT["node v, updated state"]
```

A minimal version of that update, with sum aggregation, in a form that would run against a PyTorch-style edge list:

```python
import torch

def message_pass(h, edge_index, W):
    # h: [num_nodes, dim], edge_index: [2, num_edges] as (src, dst)
    src, dst = edge_index
    messages = h[src] @ W
    out = torch.zeros_like(h)
    out.index_add_(0, dst, messages)          # order-independent: sum
    deg = torch.zeros(h.size(0)).index_add_(0, dst, torch.ones(src.size(0)))
    return out / deg.clamp(min=1).unsqueeze(-1) # mean aggregation
```

`index_add_` is exactly $\bigoplus = \text{sum}$: however `edge_index` is ordered, the result at each destination node only depends on the multiset of incoming messages, not their sequence.

## How far one layer sees, and why topology matters more than depth

Stacking $k$ message-passing layers lets information travel $k$ hops, the receptive field of a GNN is a ball of graph-distance radius $k$ around each node, the same way a stack of $3\times 3$ convolutions has a receptive field that grows with depth. What differs is how fast that ball grows, and it depends entirely on the graph's topology, not just on $k$.

On an infinite 2D grid graph the count is exact: the number of nodes within Manhattan distance $k$ of a point is $2k^2 + 2k + 1$, quadratic growth, the same shape as a CNN's receptive field. On a graph with small-world structure, the kind most real social, citation, or interaction networks have, the neighborhood count grows roughly exponentially with $k$ until it saturates at the size of the whole graph, which is the graph-theoretic content behind "six degrees of separation." The chart below illustrates the shape of that difference on a schematic branching factor, it is not measured on a specific dataset:

```plot
{
  "data": [
    {"x": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "y": [5, 13, 25, 41, 61, 85], "type": "scatter", "mode": "lines+markers", "name": "2D grid, exact: 2k\u00b2+2k+1"},
    {"x": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "y": [6, 36, 216, 1000, 1000, 1000], "type": "scatter", "mode": "lines+markers", "name": "small-world, schematic, branching \u22486, capped at 1000 nodes"}
  ],
  "layout": {
    "title": "Receptive field size vs message-passing depth (illustrative, not measured)",
    "xaxis": {"title": "layers / hops (k)"},
    "yaxis": {"title": "reachable nodes", "type": "log"}
  }
}
```

The practical consequence is that "just add more layers" behaves completely differently depending on what the graph looks like: on grid-like structure it's the same safe move as deepening a CNN, on small-world structure a handful of layers already reaches most of the graph, and going deeper mixes in noise from unrelated nodes faster than it adds useful signal, this is most of the reason GNNs tend to be shallow (2 to 4 layers) compared to CNNs.

## Past permutations: continuous groups

Point clouds and molecules don't just need permutation equivariance for which atom is atom 1 versus atom 2, they also need equivariance to $SE(3)$, since rotating and translating the whole structure in space shouldn't change what the network predicts about it, only how the predicted geometry is oriented. The features that transform correctly under rotation are built from spherical harmonics $Y_l^m$, which transform under a rotation $R$ by the Wigner-D matrix of the corresponding degree:

$$
Y_l^m(Rx) = \sum_{m'=-l}^{l} D^{l}_{m'm}(R)\, Y_l^{m'}(x)
$$

instead of forcing every feature down to a rotation-invariant scalar immediately (which throws away directional information), these "steerable" features keep an explicit, bookkept relationship to the rotation group all the way through the network, and only get collapsed to invariants at the very end, where the task actually calls for it. This is the same principle behind keeping orientation estimates in $SO(3)$ rather than flattening them into an ordinary vector before combining them, described in the [earlier fusion note](post.html?slug=geodesic-averaging-demo), it's the same failure mode, just noticed on the equivariance-first side of the literature rather than arrived at independently from a tracking problem.

## The pattern underneath

Every case above answers the same question: what does this data stay the same under, and how do I make sure the model respects that instead of quietly breaking it? Grids get translation, graphs get permutation, point clouds get rotation, meshes get local reparametrization. Picking the architecture is really just picking which group's action the data actually has, then building layers that commute with it. Getting that match wrong doesn't usually cause an obvious failure, it shows up as a model that needs far more data or far more augmentation than it should, to learn by brute force something the architecture could have gotten for free.

posts.title: Demo post: math, diagrams, and plots in markdown
posts.summary: A template post showing every supported block: KaTeX math, a Mermaid diagram, a Plotly chart, and a code block. Delete once you've read data/posts.json and README.md.
posts.body:
## Why rotations don't average

A rotation in 3D lives on $SO(3)$, not in $\mathbb{R}^3$. Averaging two rotation matrices entrywise and re-orthogonalizing does not give the rotation that sits "between" them in any sense that respects the group structure:

$$\bar{R} \neq \frac{R_1 + R_2}{2}$$

That chordal average is a common shortcut, and it's wrong exactly where it matters, at large angular separations, where linear interpolation cuts through the interior of the rotation group instead of following a geodesic on it.

### Geodesic averaging instead

Average in the tangent space at the identity, via the matrix logarithm, then map back with the exponential:

$$\bar{R} = \exp\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\log(R_1 R_2^{-1})\right) R_2$$

First order approximation to the true geodesic mean, but cheap and correct for the angular ranges this project actually sees.

### Where it sits in the fusion pipeline

Mermaid node and edge labels can carry real KaTeX math too, wrap the label in quotes and `$$...$$`:

```mermaid
graph LR
A["Video: pose estimate"] -->|"$$SO(3)$$"| C[Geometric fusion]
B["Audio: prosody features"] -->|"$$\mathbb{R}^n$$"| C
C --> D[Fused behavior estimate]
```

### A placeholder comparison

This chart uses made-up numbers to show the plotting syntax, not a real result yet:

```plot
{
  "data": [
    {"x": [10, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150], "y": [0.8, 2.1, 5.4, 11.2, 19.8, 31.0], "type": "scatter", "mode": "lines+markers", "name": "chordal average"},
    {"x": [10, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150], "y": [0.7, 1.5, 2.2, 2.6, 2.9, 3.1], "type": "scatter", "mode": "lines+markers", "name": "geodesic average"}
  ],
  "layout": {
    "title": "Angular error vs separation (illustrative, not real data)",
    "xaxis": {"title": "angular separation (deg)"},
    "yaxis": {"title": "error (deg)"}
  }
}
```

A code block, since posts about this kind of thing usually need one:

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.linalg import logm, expm

def geodesic_mean(R1, R2):
    return expm(0.5 * logm(R1 @ R2.T)) @ R2
```

> Replace this entire post with a real one. It exists to show every supported block type in one place: headings, inline and display math, a Mermaid diagram with math in its labels, a Plotly chart, a code block, and this blockquote.
